outlook.headline: DRAM stays tight, but incremental upside is moderating
outlook.summary: Into 2026Q1, DRAM looks set to remain fundamentally strong rather than re-accelerate: Micron still sees AI-driven pull against constrained supply growth, while Applied, Lam, and ASML all point to healthy spending around next-node and advanced patterning ramps. The key watch is whether AI/HBM tightness continues to pull broader DRAM pricing and capex higher, because mention momentum has cooled and KLA still flags DRAM cost as a downstream margin headwind even as memory-related opportunities remain intact.

drivers.label: AI-led DRAM demand remains strong
drivers.detail: AI/AI-compute demand is still the core support for DRAM, with Micron citing strong pull and Applied calling DRAM spending hot.

drivers.label: Supply growth is constrained
drivers.detail: HBM mix plus node and capacity limits are restricting supply growth, helping keep DRAM conditions tight.

drivers.label: Next-node transitions are sustaining equipment intensity
drivers.detail: 1C, DDR5, LPDDR5, EUV, and immersion adoption all indicate customers are still investing through technology transitions rather than pausing.

drivers.label: Downstream cost pressure caps broadening
drivers.detail: Higher DRAM cost is still a gross-margin headwind for some customers, suggesting the benefit is strongest upstream and may not broaden evenly across semis.

risks: If 1C/EUV and DDR5/LPDDR5 ramps slip, current spending strength could fade faster than expected.
If supply growth improves quicker than Micron expects through mix, node, or capacity gains, tightness could ease.
If DRAM cost pressure worsens for end customers, broader semiconductor demand could absorb less of the current memory strength.
